focus: Dropped H — Function Words in Connected Speech
focus_ar: حذف صوت H — الكلمات الوظيفية في الكلام المتصل
arabic_challenge: Arabic /h/ (ه) is always pronounced clearly and fully, in every position. In natural American English, the /h/ in unstressed function words — especially pronouns and auxiliary verbs like 'him', 'her', 'his', 'have', 'had' — is very often dropped completely in the middle of a sentence, while it stays fully pronounced at the start of a sentence or when stressed for emphasis.

target_sounds: dropped /h/ in pronouns: him, her, his

isolation.instructions: When 'him', 'her', or 'his' appears in the middle of a sentence and is unstressed, the /h/ often disappears, and the word links smoothly onto the word before it instead.
isolation.instructions_ar: لما him أو her أو his تظهر في وسط الجملة وبدون نبر، صوت الـ /h/ غالبًا بيختفي، والكلمة بتترابط بسلاسة مع الكلمة اللي قبلها.

pair: tell. Him. (h kept, sounds overly careful)
ask. Her. (h kept, sounds overly careful)

sentence_practice: Tell him to call her right away.
Give him the book, and ask her too.
I went with him, not for her.
daily_drills.record_this: Tell him to call her, give him the book, and ask her too — I went with him, not for her.
daily_drills.self_check: Say 'tell him' fast — did the /h/ disappear, so it sounds closer to 'tell-im'?

target_sounds: dropped /h/ in auxiliary verbs: have, has, had

isolation.instructions: In fast, unstressed speech, the auxiliary verbs 'have', 'has', and 'had' (when used as helping verbs, not main verbs) often lose their /h/, especially after a pronoun.
isolation.instructions_ar: في الكلام السريع بدون نبر، أفعال المساعدة have و has و had (لما تستخدم كأفعال مساعدة، مش كأفعال أساسية) غالبًا بتخسر صوت الـ /h/، خصوصًا بعد ضمير.

sentence_practice: I have been waiting since she has gone.
We had left before you have seen it.
He has told them what they had known.
daily_drills.record_this: I have been waiting since she has gone — we had left before you have seen it, and he has told them what they had known.
daily_drills.self_check: Do the auxiliary verbs (have/has/had) shrink and lose their /h/ when they're not the main focus of the sentence?

target_sounds: contrast: when /h/ IS pronounced (sentence-start, stress, main verb)

isolation.instructions: The /h/ stays FULLY pronounced when: (1) the word starts a sentence, (2) the word is stressed for emphasis or contrast, or (3) 'have/has/had' is used as a MAIN verb meaning 'possess', not as an auxiliary.
isolation.instructions_ar: صوت الـ /h/ بيفضل كامل لما: (١) الكلمة تبدأ الجملة، (٢) الكلمة عليها نبر للتوضيح، (٣) have/has/had مستخدمة كفعل أساسي بمعنى 'يملك'، مش كمساعد.
practice: Him? He didn't do it. (sentence-start, full H)
I told HER, not him. (stressed for contrast, full H)
I have a car. (main verb = possess, full H)

pair: I have been there. (dropped H, auxiliary)
I have a car. (full H, main verb = own)

sentence_practice: I have a car, but I have been walking lately.
I told HER the news, not him.
daily_drills.record_this: Him? He never called her. I have a car, but I have been walking lately — and I told HER the news, not him.
daily_drills.self_check: Can you identify, for each 'him/her/have' in this sentence, WHY the h stayed or dropped — position, stress, or main-verb meaning?

target_sounds: dropped H in full conversational phrases

isolation.instructions: Combine dropped-H pronouns and auxiliaries in natural, flowing phrases you'll use constantly in daily conversation.
isolation.instructions_ar: دمج ضمائر وأفعال مساعدة بدون H في عبارات طبيعية متصلة هتستخدمها كتير في المحادثة اليومية.
practice: Has she left? → Has-she-left? (her own H stays since it's the auxiliary itself, not after it)

sentence_practice: Did you tell him? Could you ask her too?
I should have known, and I would have called him.
Give him a call, and tell her the plan.
daily_drills.record_this: Did you tell him? Could you ask her too? I should have known, and I would have called him and given her a call about the plan.
daily_drills.self_check: Are the pronoun H's disappearing naturally in these connected phrases, without you having to think hard about it?

target_sounds: dropped H at natural conversational speed

isolation.instructions: Push these patterns to full natural speed, the way you'd actually speak with a friend.
isolation.instructions_ar: خد الأنماط دي لسرعة الكلام الطبيعية، بالطريقة اللي هتتكلم بيها فعلاً مع صاحبك.
practice: Slow: tell...him (H still dropping, just slower)

sentence_practice: Just tell him already, and ask her too!
I have been busy, and she has gone home.
Did you tell him? I would have called her myself.
daily_drills.record_this: Just tell him already! I have been busy, and she has gone home — did you ask her, or would you have called him yourself?
daily_drills.self_check: Record 3 times at increasing speed. Did the dropped H's hold up, or did full /h/ sounds creep back in under pressure?

daily_drills.target_sounds: Review: dropped H in pronouns, dropped H in auxiliaries, full-H contrast rules, connected phrases, natural speed
challenge_sentences: Him? I never told him — but I have been meaning to ask her about it.
She has gone, and I would have called him if I have had the time.
Did you give him the news, or should I have told her myself?
daily_drills.record_this: Him? I never told him. I have been meaning to ask her, but she has gone already — did you give him the news, or should I have told her myself?
daily_drills.self_check: For every 'him/her/have/has/had' in this passage, mark whether the H dropped or stayed, and explain why in one word (position/stress/meaning).

test_yourself.instructions: Record this passage naturally, applying dropped-H rules to pronouns and auxiliaries, while keeping full H at sentence-starts and for emphasis.
test_yourself.passage: Him? He never called her back. I told him to ask her about the meeting, but he has been too busy. She has gone to the office already, and I would have joined her if I have had more time. Did you tell him the schedule changed? I have a feeling he hasn't heard yet — could you give him a call and ask her to wait?

sounds_to_check.dropped H (pronouns, mid-sentence): told him, ask her, give him a call, ask her to wait
sounds_to_check.dropped H (auxiliaries): has been, has gone, would have, have had
sounds_to_check.full H (sentence-start / stress / main v: Him? (sentence start), I have a feeling (main verb = possess)

scoring_guide.90_100: Correct H-dropping in pronouns and auxiliaries throughout; full H correctly preserved at sentence-start, under stress, and for main-verb 'have'.
scoring_guide.70_89: Mostly correct; a few missed drops or an incorrect full-H kept where it should drop.
scoring_guide.50_69: Inconsistent — H dropping only in heavily drilled phrases.
scoring_guide.below_50: Full /h/ pronounced everywhere — go back and repeat Days 1-4.

for_arabic_speakers: This week's pattern is the OPPOSITE instinct from your first language: instead of always pronouncing ه clearly, you're learning to selectively DROP it in specific, predictable grammatical positions.
The rule is about GRAMMATICAL ROLE, not the letter itself: pronouns and auxiliary verbs drop H when unstressed; content words and sentence-initial position keep it.
This connects directly to Week 1's stress patterns — dropped H almost always happens on UNSTRESSED function words, which is the same category of word that gets reduced in general.
supplementary_notes.recording_requirement: Record the 'record_this' sentence every day. By Day 7, the dropped-H pattern should feel automatic on pronouns, not a special effort.
